ontology.foodex2_description: Cabbages and other brassica vegetables, cooked
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: Qinglong cabbage (Brassica oleracea var.) is a brassica-family leafy vegetable; closest FoodEx2 parent is cabbages and other brassica vegetables, cooked (A00LC). No exact code for this specific cultivar.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for stir-fried qinglong cabbage or its closest parent in the clinical terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of stir-fried qinglong cabbage is approximately 200 g, presented as a side dish or shared vegetable plate.

nutrient_source.title: Stir-fried Chinese cabbage (brassica leafy vegetable, cooked with oil and garlic) — adapted from USDA FoodData Central: Cabbage, chinese, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (FDC ID 169975) and adjusted for stir-frying with oil
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Qinglong cabbage (青龙菜) is a cultivar of Chinese cabbage / brassica leafy vegetable. USDA FDC entry for cooked Chinese cabbage (FDC ID 169975) was used as the closest approved base. Values were adjusted upward for fat and energy to account for stir-frying with approximately 5–8 g vegetable oil per 100 g cooked dish, consistent with standard Chinese stir-fry preparation. Sodium adjusted to reflect light seasoning with salt and soy sauce typical of restaurant preparation. Secondary cross-reference: FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition table for cooked leafy brassica vegetables.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — non-starchy vegetables category; stir-fried leafy brassica vegetable analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for qinglong cabbage specifically. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on non-starchy leafy brassica vegetables (cabbage, bok choy, Chinese greens) which consistently register GI values in the 10–20 range in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 8.4) / 100 = 1.26, rounded to 1.3. Serving size 200 g × 4.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 8.4 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.guidance: A 200 g restaurant serving provides approximately 560 mg sodium, which represents about 24% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced salt or soy sauce and limit portion size accordingly.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low net carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and only 4.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, stir-fried qinglong cabbage has a negligible glycemic load and is a suitable vegetable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, supporting blood glucose control when consumed as part of a balanced meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high fibre, and presence of cardioprotective micronutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Stir-fried qinglong cabbage is low in saturated fat (0.5 g per 100 g), contains dietary fibre (1.8 g per 100 g), vitamin C, folate, and potassium, all of which support cardiovascular health; however, sodium from restaurant preparation should be monitored in patients with established cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Qinglong Cabbage (清炒青龙菜, Qīng chǎo qīnglóng cài) is a classic Chinese vegetable dish featuring qinglong cabbage — a tender, mildly sweet leafy brassica cultivar with elongated pale-green stalks and soft dark-green leaves — cooked rapidly in a hot wok with garlic, vegetable oil, and light seasoning. The dish is a staple of Cantonese and broader Chinese home and restaurant cooking, prized for its clean, fresh flavour, slight crunch in the stalks, and silky texture in the leaves. The preparation method is 'qing chao' (清炒), meaning a pure or clean stir-fry with minimal sauce, allowing the natural sweetness and colour of the vegetable to shine. Nutritionally, the dish is low in calories (approximately 62 kcal per 100 g), very low in carbohydrates (4.2 g per 100 g), and provides modest amounts of dietary fibre, vitamin C, folate, potassium, and calcium. The fat content (approximately 4.5 g per 100 g) derives primarily from the cooking oil used in stir-frying. The glycemic index is very low (estimated GI ~15), making it an excellent vegetable choice for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate in restaurant preparations due to salt and light soy sauce seasoning. Clinically, this dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease risk, and those following weight-management or plant-based diets. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. In Singapore, it is commonly served as a shared side dish in Chinese zi char restaurants and family-style dining establishments.

confidence_reason: Qinglong cabbage is a specific Chinese brassica cultivar not individually listed in any approved primary nutrient database. Nutrient values were adapted from the closest approved USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked Chinese cabbage (FDC ID 169975) with adjustments for stir-frying oil addition. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for non-starchy leafy brassica vegetables. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact match exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA for this specific dish and cultivar.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FDC cooked Chinese cabbage (FDC ID 169975) with upward adjustment for stir-frying oil (approximately 4–5 g added fat per 100 g cooked dish). 2. Sodium (280 mg per 100 g) reflects typical restaurant-style preparation with salt and light soy sauce; home-cooked versions may have significantly lower sodium. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish; confirmed zero — plant foods contain no vitamin B12. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as leafy brassica vegetables contain negligible vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA FDC source data. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as vegetable oil used in stir-frying contains no trans fats; confirmed zero. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; naturally zero. 7. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate for non-starchy leafy brassica vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for qinglong cabbage was found in the literature. 8. The food name in the input JSON contains a minor romanization variant ('清龙菜' vs. the standard '青龙菜'); the correct Chinese characters are 青龙菜 (qīnglóng cài, meaning green dragon vegetable), and this has been corrected in the output. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and other micronutrients are estimated from USDA FDC data for cooked Chinese cabbage and are approximate. 10. All -1.0 sentinel values in the template have been replaced with estimated numeric values from the approved source; no -1 sentinel values remain in this record.
